debug_info.goal:
n : ℕ
net : BiasedHopfieldNetwork n
⊢ failed to pretty print expression (use 'set_option pp.rawOnError true' for raw representation)

verification_message: All 1 attempts failed (1 strategies x 1 attempts each)
proof_attempts:
intro x
  classical
  have h₁ : ∀ (x : HopfieldState n), ∃ (seq : BiasedUpdateSeq net x), biasedIsFixedPoint net seq.target := by
    intro x
    by_contra! h
    -- We will show that assuming no fixed point exists leads to an infinite sequence of decreasing energy values, which is impossible.
    have h₂ : ∀ (k : ℕ), ∃ (div : ℕ), biasedUpdateSeq.existsUpdate (fun (seq : BiasedUpdateSeq net x) => if seq.length = k then seq else none) < div := by
      intro k
      have h₃ := h (BiasedUpdateSeq.nil x)
      use 0
      <;> simp_all [BiasedUpdateSeq.length, BiasedUpdateSeq.nil]
      <;> aesop
    -- The above line is incorrect as a general statement, so we need to adjust it.
    -- Instead of trying to find a general contradiction, we can use the fact that the number of possible states is finite.
    have h₃ : ∃ (div : ℕ), ∀ (k : ℕ), biasedUpdateSeq.existsUpdate (fun (seq : BiasedUpdateSeq net x) => if seq.length = k then seq else none) < div := by
      -- Use the fact that the number of possible states is finite to bound the length of the sequence.
      use 2 ^ n + 1
      intro k
      have h₄ : biasedUpdateSeq.existsUpdate (fun (seq : BiasedUpdateSeq net x) => if seq.length = k then seq else none) < 2 ^ n + 1 := by
